title: Signaling for Rescue
summary: Methods to attract attention and signal for rescue
body:
## The Rule of Three

The universal distress signal is **three of anything** repeated at regular intervals:

- **Three fires** in a triangle (spaced about 30m / 100ft apart)
- **Three whistle blasts** repeated every few minutes
- **Three gunshots** spaced evenly
- **Three piles of rocks** or three bright cloth items in a clearing

Anyone trained in search and rescue recognizes groups of three as a distress signal. Space your signals at regular intervals and repeat them consistently.

## Signal Mirror

A signal mirror is one of the most effective long-range signaling tools. It can be seen from over 15km (10 miles) away in clear conditions.

### Aiming Technique

1. Hold the mirror close to your face with one hand
2. Extend your other hand at arm's length and make a **V with two fingers**
3. Place the target (aircraft, ship, distant rescuer) in the V of your fingers
4. Angle the mirror so the reflected sunlight passes through the V onto the target
5. Sweep the reflection back and forth slowly across the target's position
6. Flash in groups of three when possible

**Any reflective surface works**: CD, phone screen, aluminum foil, belt buckle, or even a credit card. A purpose-made signal mirror with a sighting hole is most effective.

## Signal Fires

### Daytime Signals

- Smoke is visible from great distances during the day
- Build a hot base fire, then add **green branches, wet leaves, or damp grass** to produce thick smoke
- **White smoke** (green vegetation) stands out against dark backgrounds (forests, dark terrain)
- **Dark smoke** (rubber, oil-soaked rags, plastic) stands out against light backgrounds (snow, sand, bright sky)
- Place signal fires in **open clearings** where smoke is not trapped by canopy

### Nighttime Signals

- Bright flames are visible for many kilometers at night
- Build three fires in a triangle pattern on the highest accessible ground
- Keep fire-starting materials dry and ready so you can ignite them quickly when you hear aircraft or see lights
- A single bright fire on a hilltop or beach can be seen from 15km (10 miles) at night

## Whistle Signals

A whistle carries **much further than the human voice** and requires less energy:

- **Three short blasts** = universal distress signal
- Repeat every 1-2 minutes
- Pause and listen for a response between sets
- A whistle can be heard up to 1.5km (1 mile) away in calm conditions compared to about 300m (1000ft) for shouting
- Whistles work when your voice fails from dehydration, cold, or exhaustion

**Always carry a whistle on your person, not buried in your pack.**

## Ground-to-Air Signals

These standard symbols are recognized by search aircraft worldwide. Make them at least **3m (10ft) tall** using contrasting materials (dark rocks on snow, bright cloth on dark ground):

- **X** = Need medical help / unable to move
- **V** = Need assistance / need supplies
- **Arrow (-->)** = Traveling in this direction
- **I** = Serious injury, need doctor
- **F** = Need food and water
- **LL** = All is well (if rescuers are looking for you)

Stamp out symbols in snow, arrange rocks or logs, tear strips of bright fabric, or trample vegetation to create contrast visible from the air.

## Morse Code SOS

SOS is the most recognized distress signal worldwide:

- **Pattern: ... --- ...** (three short, three long, three short)
- **Flashlight**: Three quick flashes, three slow flashes (hold 2-3 seconds each), three quick flashes. Pause 5 seconds. Repeat.
- **Sound**: Three quick taps, three slow taps, three quick taps on any resonant surface
- **Any medium works**: Car horn, rock on metal, banging pipes, or radio clicks

## Maximizing Your Chances

- **Signal from high ground** - Elevation dramatically increases your visible range for all methods
- **Use multiple methods simultaneously** - Combine fire, ground signals, and a mirror
- **Stay near your signal site** - Rescuers will search where they saw your signal. Do not wander.
- **Signal at regular intervals** - Aircraft may pass at predictable times. Listen for engines and be ready.
- **Contrast is everything** - Your signal must look different from the natural surroundings. Bright colors, geometric shapes, and movement all attract attention.
- **Do not give up** - Maintain your signals for as long as it takes. Rescue may come from an unexpected direction or time.
